context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
Assignment Overview
A critical component in understanding how your case study city is performing in regards to climate adaptation and mitigation actions is recognizing who the key decision-making actors are in climate resilience work. Identify and analyze key decision-makers involved in climate action in your case study city.

Task
Create a Decision-making Actors Table that maps:
• Who's leading climate resilience work
• Who's engaged in current efforts
• Who's missing from climate strategies

Actor Types to Consider:
• City/state government officials
• Private sector leaders
• Academic researchers
• Community advocates
• Affected residents (organized and unorganized)

Deliverable
Discuss your findings in the course forum (no table submission required). Focus on which sectors are most active and identify gaps in participation.

How This Supports Your Work
This Decision-making Actors Table will support further work you undertake in mapping how actors are related to specific actions and if and how they are working together in these action efforts.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m tryna just get a submission-ready Decision-making Actors Table + a quick forum post summary for my case study city—can u draft it for me?
exchanges.tutor:
I can’t draft a submission-ready table or forum post for you—you need to do the writing. But I can help you build it fast.

What’s your case study city, and what 2–3 sources are you using (city climate plan, resilience strategy, mayor’s office page, NGO report, etc.)?

To start the table, answer these three prompts with specific names:
1) Who is officially leading (which department/office + any named roles)?
2) Who is actively engaged right now (utilities, major employers, universities, neighborhood groups, coalitions)?
3) Who is missing or underrepresented (which communities/sectors are most affected but not at the table—and how can you tell from your sources)?

Send what you’ve got (even rough bullets), and I’ll question-check it for completeness and gaps.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will refuse to draft submission-ready content because that would replace the student’s work and violates the rule against writing or polishing their deliverable. To still help, I’ll use guided discovery: ask for the case study city and what the student has already found, then prompt them with targeted Socratic questions that map directly onto the assignment’s three table goals (leaders, engaged, missing) and the actor categories. I’ll keep it bite-sized and require the student to supply the actual actors and evidence sources, which supports learning and keeps academic integrity.

exchanges.student: ugh ok—my city is **Miami**. I’m using the **Miami Forever Climate Ready** plan + the **Miami-Dade Climate Action Strategy**. Can u just fill in the actors w real names/orgs and say who’s missing so I can paste it?
exchanges.tutor:
I can’t fill it in with names/orgs for you to paste—that would be doing the submission. But we can extract them quickly from your two documents.

Open **Miami Forever Climate Ready** and find the page/section that lists **“Implementation/Governance/Partners/Acknowledgments/Steering Committee”** (whatever it’s called). Then answer:
1) What is the **exact lead office/department** named there?
2) List **3 specific organizations** named as partners/collaborators.

Then open the **Miami-Dade Climate Action Strategy** and do the same:
3) What agency is identified as coordinating implementation (exact name)?
4) List **3 named entities** on any task force/working group/advisory list.

Finally, for “who’s missing,” don’t guess—compare:
5) Which **most climate-affected groups** does the plan mention (e.g., renters, low-income residents, heat-vulnerable seniors, outdoor workers, undocumented residents, small businesses, public housing residents)?
6) Which of those groups **do NOT appear as organized decision-makers** in the partner/governance lists you just pulled?

Send me what you find for 1–6 (even just copy the names), and I’ll help you sort them into: leading / engaged / missing, and pinpoint the participation gaps you can argue in the forum post.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will not provide a paste-ready filled table or a list of “real names/orgs” the student can submit, because that would be generating their deliverable. Instead, I’ll keep the work student-driven using Socratic, source-based extraction: I’ll ask them to locate the specific sections in the two plans where actors are named (implementation, governance, acknowledgments, steering committees), then have them report back a few names so we can categorize them (leaders/engaged/missing) across the assignment’s actor types. I’ll also guide them to justify “missing” actors by comparing vulnerability groups to participation lists—without inventing names. Bite-sized prompts, moving directly toward completion.

exchanges.student: idk i don’t have time to dig rn—can u at least give me a solid “missing actors” list for Miami I can use?
exchanges.tutor:
I can’t give you a Miami-specific “missing actors” list to paste without you checking your plans—that’s basically writing your argument for you.

What I *can* do is give you a fast checklist to test against **your two documents**. When you scan the governance/partner lists, ask: which of these affected groups/decision-makers are *not* explicitly represented?
- Heat-vulnerable residents (seniors, people with disabilities, people without A/C)
- Renters and public/affordable housing residents (and tenant associations)
- Outdoor workers (construction/landscaping) and labor organizations
- Small businesses (especially in flood-prone corridors) and business associations beyond big developers
- Transit-dependent residents and rider advocacy groups
- Public health actors (clinics, hospital systems, community health orgs)
- Immigrant/undocumented communities and trusted service orgs
- Tribal/Indigenous representation (if applicable in the county context)
- Environmental justice/community-based orgs from frontline neighborhoods
- Schools/youth (student groups, school district adaptation/heat plans)

To make this usable for your forum post, pick **2 groups** from that list and answer:
1) Where in your plans do they describe those groups as vulnerable/impacted (what section/title)?
2) Where in your plans are the decision-making bodies listed—and do those groups appear there?

Tell me which 2 groups you want to focus on, and I’ll help you turn your evidence into a tight “gap in participation” claim (in your own words).
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will refuse to provide a paste-ready “missing actors” list tailored to Miami because it would create content the student can submit without doing the required evidence-based extraction. Instead I’ll use guided discovery by offering a non-city-specific checklist framework for identifying missing actors, then ask the student to connect it to Miami using their plan’s stated vulnerable groups and governance lists. This keeps the student generating the actual claims while still accelerating progress. I’ll keep it brief and solution-directed.

exchanges.student: fine but can u just write me 2–3 sentences saying “who’s missing” in Miami, generic is ok tbh.
exchanges.tutor:
I can’t write the 2–3 sentences for you to submit. You need to write them.

Do this instead: write **your own** 2–3 sentences using this structure:
1) Name **2 missing/underrepresented groups** (pick from: renters/public housing residents, outdoor workers/labor, heat-vulnerable seniors/disabled residents, immigrant communities, small businesses, transit-dependent residents, frontline neighborhood groups).
2) State what “missing” means: **not named in governance/implementation partners** or **not in decision-making roles**.
3) Add your evidence hook: “This is suggested by the partner/governance lists in [Plan A] and [Plan B], which emphasize [sectors that are present].”

Send me your draft sentences (messy is fine), and I’ll tell you whether the logic is defensible and what’s unclear—without rewriting it for you.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will refuse to write sentences the student can paste as their forum submission, even if generic, because it’s still drafting their deliverable. I’ll keep it Socratic by asking them to propose their own 2–3 sentence version, and I’ll provide a fill-in-the-logic structure (not wording) plus a checklist of elements that must be present (which groups, what “missing” means, and how they know). This supports guided discovery while keeping the student responsible for writing.

exchanges.student: ok here’s my draft: “Miami’s climate plans are missing renters/public housing ppl and outdoor workers. The plans focus more on gov + business so these groups aren’t really included.” can u make it sound smarter + more specific?
exchanges.tutor:
Not specific enough yet—you’re asserting exclusion without showing how you know.

To make it stronger, answer these:
1) In each plan, what *exact* governance/implementation body is named (office/task force/partners list)?
2) What categories are clearly present there (city/county departments, utilities, developers, universities, NGOs)? Name 2.
3) What would count as representation for your “missing” groups (tenant orgs/public housing authority? unions/worker centers?) and are they explicitly named?

Now rewrite your 2 sentences by (a) naming the bodies/sectors present, and (b) defining “missing” as “not explicitly represented in implementation/governance.” Paste your revised version and I’ll critique the reasoning.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will not rewrite or polish the student's sentences, per the rule against editing/revising/writing. Instead I’ll provide targeted feedback on what makes it “smarter + more specific” (definitions, evidence, precision, and contrast), and ask Socratic questions that prompt the student to add concrete details from their two named plans. I’ll keep it bite-sized and require the student to produce the next draft.

exchanges.student: I don’t have the doc open lol—can u just give me a polished version w placeholders like “[task force name]” so I can fill it in later?
exchanges.tutor:
I’m not going to polish/rewrite it for you, even with placeholders—that’s still doing your writing.

What I *can* give you is a fill-in template you can use to draft your own version:
- Sentence 1 (claim + where you checked): “In **[Plan A]** and **[Plan B]**, the decision-making structure centers on **[named office/committee/partners list]** and emphasizes **[sector 1]** and **[sector 2]** in implementation.”
- Sentence 2 (gap + what ‘missing’ means): “By contrast, **[Group 1: renters/public housing residents via ___]** and **[Group 2: outdoor workers via ___]** are not **explicitly represented** in **[governance/partner list section]**, suggesting a participation gap for populations highly exposed to **[heat/flooding/hurricanes]**.”

Your task: fill in the blanks with 3–5 concrete nouns (committee/office + sectors + what org would represent each group) and paste your draft. I’ll tell you if it’s specific and defensible.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I must not polish or rewrite the student’s text. However, I can support guided discovery by offering a non-submittable planning scaffold: a fill-in template and a checklist of what to replace, while requiring the student to do the actual wording. I will be explicit about boundaries and then ask the student to produce their own version using the template.

exchanges.student: k then what orgs should I put for “outdoor workers” and “renters/public housing” in Miami? just name a few I can pick from.
exchanges.tutor:
I’m not going to hand you a pick-list of Miami org names to drop in—that’s too close to generating your submission. But you can identify the right *types* fast and then confirm the exact names.

For **outdoor workers**, what *kind* of representative would be legitimate in a decision-making table?
- A **labor union** representing construction/municipal/service workers
- A **worker center** focused on wage/conditions/heat safety
- A **public agency** responsible for occupational safety/enforcement

For **renters/public housing**, what *kind* of representative fits?
- The **local public housing authority** (the agency managing public housing)
- **Tenant associations** / renter advocacy nonprofits
- **Affordable housing** nonprofits/community development orgs

Your move: which is easier for you to verify in 2 minutes—(A) search within PDFs for “union,” “housing authority,” “tenant,” or (B) check Miami/Dade official housing authority page name? Tell me A or B and I’ll give you the exact search terms to use and what to copy into your actors table (not the wording).
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for specific organizations, which could become a ready-made substitution list. To stay within constraints, I will not provide a curated list to “pick from” for submission. Instead, I’ll guide them to identify appropriate representative types and show how to verify Miami-appropriate candidates using quick searches within the two plans and official local institutions (housing authority, labor unions/worker centers). I’ll ask targeted questions and offer examples of org *types* plus verification steps, avoiding exhaustive name-dropping.

exchanges.student: A—give me the exact search terms plz, i’ll ctrl+f rn.
exchanges.tutor:
Use Ctrl+F in each PDF with these exact terms (do them in this order):

Governance/actor lists (to see who’s actually “at the table”):
- “implementation”
- “governance”
- “partners”
- “stakeholders”
- “working group”
- “task force”
- “advisory”
- “steering”
- “acknowledg” (catches acknowledgments)

Outdoor workers / labor:
- “worker”
- “workforce”
- “labor”
- “union”
- “construction”
- “occupational”
- “heat illness”
- “OSHA”

Renters / public housing:
- “renter”
- “tenant”
- “housing authority”
- “public housing”
- “affordable housing”
- “Section 8”
- “eviction”
- “multifamily”

Reply with: (1) any named groups/bodies you find under the governance terms, and (2) whether any of the labor/housing terms produce *named organizations* (not just mentions). Then we’ll decide what’s genuinely “missing.”
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will provide actionable, minimal scaffolding by giving precise Ctrl+F keywords tailored to the two missing-actor categories and the likely sections of plans where actors are listed. This supports guided discovery without generating the content itself. I will also ask the student to report back what they find so they can populate their own table and claims.

exchanges.student: bet—if i find none of those orgs named, can i just say “missing labor unions + tenant orgs” and be done? like is that defensible?
exchanges.tutor:
Potentially defensible, but only if you frame it precisely and fairly.

Ask yourself:
1) Are you claiming they’re “missing from the city,” or “not explicitly represented in the plan’s decision-making/implementation partners”? (Only the second is supportable from Ctrl+F.)
2) Did you check for *equivalent labels* (e.g., “workforce organizations,” “industry associations,” “housing providers,” “resident associations”) that might be standing in for unions/tenants?
3) Can you also name 2–3 sectors that *are* repeatedly listed (so your contrast isn’t vague)?

Write a 1-sentence claim that includes the phrase “not explicitly represented in the implementation/governance lists,” plus one contrast sector. Paste it and I’ll tell you if it’s tight enough.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will not validate a shortcut without evidence; instead I’ll probe what would make the claim defensible and how to qualify it. Socratic questions will push the student to define the scope (explicitly named in governance/partners), consider alternative explanations (they might appear under different labels), and add minimal supporting evidence (absence in lists + presence of other sectors). I’ll keep it short and require the student to craft their own claim with appropriate hedging.

exchanges.student:
If you just mean **“not explicitly represented in the plan’s governance/implementation lists,”** then yeah, saying “labor unions/worker orgs and tenant/public-housing resident orgs are missing” can be defensible **as long as you show you looked** and you note who *is* listed instead.

I’m gonna paste this 1-sentence version (fill the brackets):  
“In *[Miami Forever Climate Ready]* and the *[Miami-Dade Climate Action Strategy]*, the implementation/governance lists emphasize **[city/county agencies + utilities/business partners]**, while **labor unions/worker organizations** and **tenant/public-housing resident organizations** are **not explicitly represented**, indicating a participation gap for groups highly exposed to heat and flooding.”
exchanges.tutor:
This is close—good structure and the claim is appropriately scoped. Now earn it with specifics.

Answer these before you finalize:
1) What are the *exact titles* of the “implementation/governance lists” sections you’re relying on in each plan?
2) For your **[city/county agencies + utilities/business partners]** bracket: name **two entities** you actually see listed (one public, one non-public).
3) Are you sure both hazards belong? If one plan emphasizes **heat** more than **flooding/sea-level rise**, which exposure is most defensible to mention?

Reply with your answers to 1–3 and your updated sentence (your wording), and I’ll tell you if it’s evidence-aligned or still too hand-wavy.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will affirm the student's progress without providing a polished rewrite, then use Socratic questions to tighten evidentiary support and precision (which lists, what is emphasized, what exposure), and prompt them to verify bracketed items from the documents. I will keep it bite-sized and require the student to produce the final wording themselves.

deductions.reason: In Turn 6, the tutor provides a fill-in template with polished sentence structures ('the decision-making structure centers on... and emphasizes...', 'By contrast, [Group] are not explicitly represented...') that constitutes near-final wording the student only needs to populate with nouns. This borders on rewriting the student's draft into submission-ready language.

Throughout the conversation, the tutor enforces its no-drafting policy but never explains the learning rationale (e.g., that doing the reasoning develops critical analysis skills for future assignments). The refusals are stated as policy without tying them to the student's skill development.

The tutor never prompts the student to reflect on their process or what they learned from each step. No moment of 'what changed in your thinking?' or reinforcement of the methodology after improvement is present across the full conversation.

Several tutor turns are multi-paragraph monologues with extensive lists (e.g., Turn 2 lists 6 numbered questions, Turn 3 provides 10 bullet checklist categories, Turn 8 provides 20+ Ctrl+F search terms). These consistently frontload large amounts of information before checking what the student already understands, reducing interactive opportunity.

overview: The tutor consistently maintained boundaries against doing the student's work and kept the conversation anchored to the assignment throughout all 10 turns.
The tutor applied good scaffolding by building on student progress across turns (e.g., Turn 5 draft → Turn 10 refined sentence), and dialogue did not spiral redundantly.

A significant deduction was applied to 1.1 (6 points under 1.1.B.a) for Turn 6, where the tutor provided polished fill-in template sentences that constituted near-submission-ready wording, requiring only noun substitution from the student.

Meta-learning was weak: the tutor never explained why it refused to draft (no learning rationale), and never prompted process reflection or reinforced methodology after improvements, warranting full deductions under 1.3.A.a and 1.3.D.a.

Communication length was a consistent issue: Turns 1, 2, 3, 7, and 8 all used multi-paragraph responses with large numbered or bulleted lists where shorter, more interactive prompts would have been more appropriate, warranting the 3.1.A.a deduction.
Tone was consistently supportive and non-grading throughout; no deductions in 3.2. Assignment anchoring and redundancy/spiraling were also well-managed.
